model_narrative: We run a multi-echelon supply chain over a sequence of planning periods. The chain has several stages running from an upstream source down to the final customer-facing stage. In every period and at every stage we decide how much to reorder from the stage upstream, how much inventory to hold, how much to ship downstream, and how much demand to leave unmet as backlog. Reorders take a known number of periods to arrive, sales earn a stage-specific price, holding inventory and carrying backlog both incur costs, and reordering costs money. The objective is to maximize total discounted profit summed across all periods, where profit earned later is worth less by a fixed per-period discount factor.

sets.name: N
sets.doc: the planning periods, numbered from zero; these are the periods in which decisions are made

sets.name: N1
sets.doc: the planning periods plus one extra endpoint period, used to index inventory levels that are carried into the period after each decision period

sets.name: M
sets.doc: all stages of the supply chain, ordered from the most upstream stage zero to the most downstream final stage

sets.name: M0
sets.doc: the stages that carry inventory, which is every stage except the final downstream stage

params.name: up
params.doc: the sales price earned per unit at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: uc
params.doc: the replenishment cost paid per unit reordered at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: dc
params.doc: the penalty cost charged per unit of unfulfilled demand carried as backlog at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: hc
params.doc: the inventory holding cost charged per unit held at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: sc
params.doc: the production or supply capacity at each inventory-carrying stage, the most that may be reordered there in a period

params.name: lt
params.doc: the lead time at each inventory-carrying stage, the whole number of periods a reorder placed at that stage takes to arrive

params.name: dis
params.doc: the per-period discount factor applied to profit, a number between zero and one; a profit earned in period n is discounted by this factor raised to the power n

params.name: dmd
params.doc: the customer demand seen at the most downstream stage in each period, in units

vars.name: i
vars.doc: on-hand inventory held at each inventory-carrying stage entering each period; the entries for the opening period are fixed initial inventory and are not decisions

vars.name: t
vars.doc: pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage entering each period; the entries for the opening period are fixed at zero and are not decisions

vars.name: r
vars.doc: the reorder quantity placed at each inventory-carrying stage in each period

vars.name: s
vars.doc: the units sold or shipped out of each stage in each period

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the backlog of unfulfilled demand carried at each stage in each period

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the discounted profit realized in each period

constraints.name: lt[m]))
constraints.intent: Track on-hand inventory at each inventory-carrying stage from one period to the next. For each such stage and period, the inventory carried into the following period equals the inventory carried into the current period, plus any reorder that was placed earlier and has now arrived after its lead time has elapsed, minus what is shipped out of the stage this period. When not enough periods have passed for a reorder to have arrived yet, no arriving quantity is added.

constraints.name: lt[0]))
constraints.intent: Sales out of the most upstream stage in each period cannot exceed what is physically available there. For each period, the units sold from the upstream stage must not exceed the inventory on hand at that stage plus any reorder that was placed earlier and has now arrived after its lead time. Before any reorder has had time to arrive, only the on-hand inventory is available.

constraints.name: b[n-1,
constraints.intent: Sales out of the most upstream stage in each period are also limited by the demand it faces together with any demand left unmet earlier. For each period, the units sold from the upstream stage must not exceed that period's demand plus the backlog carried over from the previous period. In the very first period there is no earlier backlog, so the limit is just that period's demand.

constraints.name: s[n,
constraints.intent: Link sales out of each downstream stage to the reorders placed by the stage just below it. For every stage other than the most upstream one, and in each period, the units sold out of that stage equal the units reordered in the same period by the next stage down the chain. The most upstream stage is not governed by this rule.

constraints.name: r[n,
constraints.intent: Reorders are limited by available capacity. For each inventory-carrying stage and each period, the quantity reordered must not exceed that stage's supply capacity.

constraints.name: r[n,
constraints.intent: A reorder placed at the most upstream stage cannot draw more than the supplying stage holds. For each period, the quantity reordered at the most upstream stage must not exceed the on-hand inventory available at the next stage up the chain.

constraints.name: lt[m]))
constraints.intent: Track pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage from one period to the next. For each such stage and period, the in-transit quantity carried into the following period equals the in-transit quantity carried into the current period, minus any reorder that has now arrived after its lead time, plus the new reorder placed this period. Before any reorder has had time to arrive, nothing is subtracted for arrivals.

constraints.name: dmd[n]
constraints.intent: Track the backlog of unfulfilled demand at each stage from one period to the next. For each stage and period, the backlog at the end of the period equals the new requirement arriving at that stage plus any backlog carried over from the previous period, minus what is sold to satisfy it this period. At the most upstream stage the arriving requirement is the customer demand, while at every other stage it is the reorder placed by the next stage down. In the very first period there is no earlier backlog to carry over.

constraints.name: M.at(len(
constraints.intent: Define the discounted profit realized in each period. For each period, take the sales revenue earned across all stages, then subtract the cost of all reorders placed plus the purchase cost of the final stage's sales, subtract the penalty for all backlogged demand, and subtract the holding cost on the inventory carried into the next period. Discount the whole amount by the per-period discount factor raised to the power of how many periods have elapsed, and set the period's profit equal to that discounted net amount.

query: Procurement wants to smooth out ordering at the retail stage. We're looking at a rule that holds each period's retail reorder to no more than 20 units. What does that do to the plan's profit?
expected_pyomo: model.smooth_retail = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: m.r[n, 0] <= 20)

In every period and at every stage we decide how much to reorder from the stage upstream, how much inventory to hold, how much to ship downstream, and how much demand to leave unmet as backlog. Reorders take a known number of periods to arrive, sales earn a stage-specific price, holding inventory and carrying backlog both incur costs, and reordering costs money. vars.name: t
vars.doc: pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage entering each period; the entries for the opening period are fixed at zero and are not decisions

constraints.name: M.at(len(

query: Finance is pushing a working-capital target. There's a proposal on the table to keep total on-hand inventory across the stages under 250 units in every period after the first. Plug that in and tell me the resulting profit.
expected_pyomo: model.lean_inventory = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: sum(m.i[n, s] for s in m.M0) <= 250 if n >= 1 else Constraint.Skip)

query: The board is pitching a leaner total-procurement budget. Suppose we cap the retail stage's reorders summed across the whole horizon at 400 units. What income does the optimizer return under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.budget_retail = Constraint(expr=sum(model.r[n, 0] for n in model.N) <= 400)

expected_pyomo: model.safety_retail = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: m.i[n, 0] >= 30 if n >= 1 else Constraint.Skip)

constraints.intent: Reorders are limited by available capacity. For each inventory-carrying stage and each period, the quantity reordered must not exceed that stage's supply capacity.

constraints.name: r[n,
constraints.intent: A reorder placed at the most upstream stage cannot draw more than the supplying stage holds. For each period, the quantity reordered at the most upstream stage must not exceed the on-hand inventory available at the next stage up the chain.

constraints.name: lt[m]))
constraints.intent: Track pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage from one period to the next. For each such stage and period, the in-transit quantity carried into the following period equals the in-transit quantity carried into the current period, minus any reorder that has now arrived after its lead time, plus the new reorder placed this period. Before any reorder has had time to arrive, nothing is subtracted for arrivals.

constraints.name: dmd[n]
constraints.intent: Track the backlog of unfulfilled demand at each stage from one period to the next. For each stage and period, the backlog at the end of the period equals the new requirement arriving at that stage plus any backlog carried over from the previous period, minus what is sold to satisfy it this period. At the most upstream stage the arriving requirement is the customer demand, while at every other stage it is the reorder placed by the next stage down. In the very first period there is no earlier backlog to carry over.

constraints.name: M.at(len(
constraints.intent: Define the discounted profit realized in each period. For each period, take the sales revenue earned across all stages, then subtract the cost of all reorders placed plus the purchase cost of the final stage's sales, subtract the penalty for all backlogged demand, and subtract the holding cost on the inventory carried into the next period. Discount the whole amount by the per-period discount factor raised to the power of how many periods have elapsed, and set the period's profit equal to that discounted net amount.

query: The plan books a loss in the early periods, which my CFO finds hard to defend. Shouldn't every period at least break even on profit? What's the model seeing that I'm not when it lets those opening periods run in the red?
expected_pyomo: model.nonneg_profit = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: m.p[n] >= 0)

model_narrative: We run a multi-echelon supply chain over a sequence of planning periods. The chain has several stages running from an upstream source down to the final customer-facing stage. In every period and at every stage we decide how much to reorder from the stage upstream, how much inventory to hold, how much to ship downstream, and how much demand to leave unmet as backlog. Reorders take a known number of periods to arrive, sales earn a stage-specific price, holding inventory and carrying backlog both incur costs, and reordering costs money. The objective is to maximize total discounted profit summed across all periods, where profit earned later is worth less by a fixed per-period discount factor.

sets.name: N
sets.doc: the planning periods, numbered from zero; these are the periods in which decisions are made

sets.name: N1
sets.doc: the planning periods plus one extra endpoint period, used to index inventory levels that are carried into the period after each decision period

sets.name: M
sets.doc: all stages of the supply chain, ordered from the most upstream stage zero to the most downstream final stage

sets.name: M0
sets.doc: the stages that carry inventory, which is every stage except the final downstream stage

params.name: up
params.doc: the sales price earned per unit at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: uc
params.doc: the replenishment cost paid per unit reordered at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: dc
params.doc: the penalty cost charged per unit of unfulfilled demand carried as backlog at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: hc
params.doc: the inventory holding cost charged per unit held at each stage, in dollars per unit

params.name: sc
params.doc: the production or supply capacity at each inventory-carrying stage, the most that may be reordered there in a period

params.name: lt
params.doc: the lead time at each inventory-carrying stage, the whole number of periods a reorder placed at that stage takes to arrive

params.name: dis
params.doc: the per-period discount factor applied to profit, a number between zero and one; a profit earned in period n is discounted by this factor raised to the power n

params.name: dmd
params.doc: the customer demand seen at the most downstream stage in each period, in units

vars.name: i
vars.doc: on-hand inventory held at each inventory-carrying stage entering each period; the entries for the opening period are fixed initial inventory and are not decisions

vars.name: t
vars.doc: pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage entering each period; the entries for the opening period are fixed at zero and are not decisions

vars.name: r
vars.doc: the reorder quantity placed at each inventory-carrying stage in each period

vars.name: s
vars.doc: the units sold or shipped out of each stage in each period

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the backlog of unfulfilled demand carried at each stage in each period

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the discounted profit realized in each period

constraints.name: lt[m]))
constraints.intent: Track on-hand inventory at each inventory-carrying stage from one period to the next. For each such stage and period, the inventory carried into the following period equals the inventory carried into the current period, plus any reorder that was placed earlier and has now arrived after its lead time has elapsed, minus what is shipped out of the stage this period. When not enough periods have passed for a reorder to have arrived yet, no arriving quantity is added.

constraints.name: lt[0]))
constraints.intent: Sales out of the most upstream stage in each period cannot exceed what is physically available there. For each period, the units sold from the upstream stage must not exceed the inventory on hand at that stage plus any reorder that was placed earlier and has now arrived after its lead time. Before any reorder has had time to arrive, only the on-hand inventory is available.

constraints.name: b[n-1,
constraints.intent: Sales out of the most upstream stage in each period are also limited by the demand it faces together with any demand left unmet earlier. For each period, the units sold from the upstream stage must not exceed that period's demand plus the backlog carried over from the previous period. In the very first period there is no earlier backlog, so the limit is just that period's demand.

constraints.name: s[n,
constraints.intent: Link sales out of each downstream stage to the reorders placed by the stage just below it. For every stage other than the most upstream one, and in each period, the units sold out of that stage equal the units reordered in the same period by the next stage down the chain. The most upstream stage is not governed by this rule.

constraints.name: r[n,
constraints.intent: Reorders are limited by available capacity. For each inventory-carrying stage and each period, the quantity reordered must not exceed that stage's supply capacity.

constraints.name: r[n,
constraints.intent: A reorder placed at the most upstream stage cannot draw more than the supplying stage holds. For each period, the quantity reordered at the most upstream stage must not exceed the on-hand inventory available at the next stage up the chain.

constraints.name: lt[m]))
constraints.intent: Track pipeline inventory in transit toward each inventory-carrying stage from one period to the next. For each such stage and period, the in-transit quantity carried into the following period equals the in-transit quantity carried into the current period, minus any reorder that has now arrived after its lead time, plus the new reorder placed this period. Before any reorder has had time to arrive, nothing is subtracted for arrivals.

constraints.name: dmd[n]
constraints.intent: Track the backlog of unfulfilled demand at each stage from one period to the next. For each stage and period, the backlog at the end of the period equals the new requirement arriving at that stage plus any backlog carried over from the previous period, minus what is sold to satisfy it this period. At the most upstream stage the arriving requirement is the customer demand, while at every other stage it is the reorder placed by the next stage down. In the very first period there is no earlier backlog to carry over.

constraints.name: M.at(len(
constraints.intent: Define the discounted profit realized in each period. For each period, take the sales revenue earned across all stages, then subtract the cost of all reorders placed plus the purchase cost of the final stage's sales, subtract the penalty for all backlogged demand, and subtract the holding cost on the inventory carried into the next period. Discount the whole amount by the per-period discount factor raised to the power of how many periods have elapsed, and set the period's profit equal to that discounted net amount.

query: The reorder quantities jump around from period to period and it makes planning a headache. Couldn't we just lock each stage's reorder to one steady number across the whole horizon? What's keeping the model from running a level ordering pattern?
expected_pyomo: model.level_reorders = Constraint(model.N, model.M0, rule=lambda m, n, s: m.r[n, s] == m.r[0, s] if n >= 1 else Constraint.Skip)

query: Warehousing keeps complaining that total stock sits high across the stages. Wouldn't it be obvious to hold the combined on-hand inventory under 250 units every period? Walk me through what's pulling the plan toward carrying more.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_total_inventory = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: sum(m.i[n, s] for s in m.M0) <= 250 if n >= 1 else Constraint.Skip)

query: Retail sales swing all over the place period to period, which makes the storefront hard to staff. Shouldn't we just hold the retail sales volume flat across the horizon? What's the tradeoff the model is chasing by letting it vary?
expected_pyomo: model.level_retail_sales = Constraint(model.N, rule=lambda m, n: m.s[n, 0] == m.s[0, 0] if n >= 1 else Constraint.Skip)
